Washington College Championships

by Mike Schuh

Inspired by the success of Eric Bone and the UW team at last year’s US Intercollegiate Championships, I suggested that Cascade OC host a state intercollegiate event this year. This was accepted by the COC board, especially when I said I would be the meet director. The date was set to follow the WIOL championships and to be a month or so before the national championships. Lake Tradition was chosen as being most suitable, with challenging terrain and a large area.

Intercollegiate scoring was derived from the national rules, with several simplifications: the Washington State event would be just one day instead of two, both men and women would be on the same course, team scores would be the total time of the fastest three finishers from each school, and anyone attending a Washington State post-secondary school full time would be eligible.

There were 14 participants from six schools. To no one’s surprise, the UW team, comprised of half of last year’s national champion team, took the first three places and the team title. As no other school had three finishers, second place went to Western Washington University, the only school with two finishers. Third place went to Pacific Lutheran University.

Among the individuals, Rune Hoel edged out Eric Bone for the men. For the women, Lisa Nordlund and high school class mate Ellen Boomer (now attending different colleges) were the only entrants with Lisa, a top WIOL participant a few years back, getting to the finish faster.

Of the 14 participants, four (Eric, Tyler, Lisa, and Ellen) are WIOL “alumni”. Dan Waugh, ever the promoter of orienteering (and founder of WIOL) brought out four members of the UW’s Russia and Eastern Europe program. And, to round out the international flavor of the proceedings, there were three Norwegian students as well—Rune, Kjell, and Harald.

All in all, the meet went quite well. The team and women's competition might not have been keen, but this is the first year for this event, and I hope that it grow in the future. My thanks to all who took part and to the meet staff: Dave Petty, registration; Marti Anderson and Terry Loving, timing; Jackie Ritmire, course 1 design and setting; Neil and Lael Smith, courses 2 and 3 design and setting; Bruce McAlister for providing refreshments; Debbie Newell, awards and vetting; Fred Schmitt, vetting; and Eric Bone, Bill Cusworth, Rune Hoel, Lisa Nordlund, Joseph Ho, Seth Brothers, and Jackie Ritmire for picking up markers. Thank you!

1994 WIOL Championships

by Mike Schuh

The Twelfth Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League Championships were held at Seattle’s Seward Park on February 12th. Competition was keen on the A course, and on the B course as well. Bremerton High School took the team honors, partly due to their large numbers (a bus load...) but also to Terry Bryant’s coaching.

Last year’s champions, David Thatcher and Rachell Tharp, were both seniors, leaving this year's title open. This year’s boy’s championships did seem to echo last year’s, however: Nicholas Bone beat Fred Schmitt, both ahead of Chris Schreiner and David Stainsby (last year, David placed ahead of Chris). That was on the A course. On the reinstated B course, rookie Chris Hooyman blew past everyone and won with a margin of nearly seven minutes. Second place Randy Coe improved mightily from last year and finished more than a minute ahead of teammate George Alina.

For the girls, Maia Wright was the only one to attempt the A course, earning her the title of champion

(she placed in the middle of an otherwise all male field). On the B course, Joan Maza was the first girl, placing fifth overall. Christine Matter and Kari Jones took second and third, with Kari having just 30 seconds over teammate Heather Daniels.

For the middle school students, Derek Schmitt repeated as champion. Second was Lael Smith, and Nathan Schmitt was third. Last year, Lael ran in a public category on the same course as the Nathan, but not quite as quickly. Sadly, this year Nathan copied the wrong set of control codes onto his control card, and lost a lot of time on the course. On the bright side, both Nathan and Lael are seventh graders, so they can look forward to five more WIOL championships and, presumably, a few rematches.

No girls took part in the middle school competition.

The only time Angela and Nicholas Tallent took part in WIOL events this season was at the championship where, uncontested, they became the grade school champions. Last year they finished in a tie; this year Angela (a former national age group champion) was faster than her little brother. I suspect that she won't be able to do that too much longer...

There were a few problems at the championships. The worst was Chris Hooyman getting a B course map instead of one for the A course. I allowed him to run the A course later (which he completed in about 41 minutes, two minutes better than Nicholas Bone), but upon reconsideration decided that this wouldn't be fair to the others who competed on the A course. A true sportsman, Chris accepted first place on the B course—and he'll be back next year as a senior.

There was some confusion as to which course was for which participants; next year we will try to be more clear about this (Nathan Schmitt's problems were a result of this). Another problem came from the design of the WIOL B course. The best route to control 1 passed near control 5, which proved to be too great a temptation for a couple of participants. At control 3 on the B course (which was control 5 on the A course) control cards were checked and two competitors had visited controls out order, which is against the rules. A slight change in the design of the course might have prevented this problem.

In general, the event was a good one, and I would like to thank the many individuals who helped make it so: assistant meet director Paul Magnus; mappers Bill Cusworth and Eric Bone (Eric, 1991 and 1992 WIOL champion, also designed and set the courses); starters Marti Anderson, Walt Estep, and Toko Magnus; finish crew David Keyes, Toko Magnus, Mark Howlett, Rick Hood; registration workers Aaron Charlop, Harvey Friedman, and Jim Ley; awards by Debbie Newell; WIOL registration Ardis Dull and Mark Anderson; refreshments by Bruce McAlister; and general assistance by Rick Hood, Tric Charlop, and Margie Bone. I hope I mentioned everyone...

Thank you everyone, and we'll see you in November for next year’s league!

Team REECAS Stuns Sports World

"No Dan O'Briens here!" shouted Paul McClung as he vaulted over a moss-encrusted deadfall and disappeared into a swamp, while leading Team REECAS to stunning near upset of the national champion UW Husky Orienteering team in the Washington Intercollegiate Championship. The REECAS team came out of nowhere in the narrowest finish since the inception of this classic event, with McClung followed by closely by Ben Hanson, Dobek Pater and Rob Smurr.

The medal winning performance was especially satisfying, in the view of the fact that the main opposition, the Husky team, not only fielded the defending national intercollegiate champion but had added a Norwegian ringer, who eked out a win on the 5-kilometer course on the Lake Tradition plateau above the once sleepy town of Issaquah.

When interviewed by CNN after the competition, the team coach, Dan Waugh, who also ran, lauded his runners. "They're compete on a level of those with years more experience," he said, "and in rough terrain." Elaborating, with reference to his patch and torn orienteering suit, gaping scratches in his thighs and muddy ankles, Waugh went on, "Orienteering is like a metaphor for being a program chair at a university—a struggle with limited resources, thorny problems, and a lot of s--t. If it weren't for the inspiration of this outstanding group of graduate students...."

Team REECAS has truly an uphill fight in its quest for the championship. Recalcitrant meet officials tried to award its medal to another school whose "team" consisted of one individual. Worse, one of the team’s outstanding performers, Lorca Fitschen, was sidelined by an end-of-quarter term-paper assigned by an East European Historian notorious for his demands on students. When reached at her home, Lorca commented, "It was like a well-aimed blow to the knee with an aluminum truncheon. I kept asking, why me, why me? Why now?" Rumor has it that the professor in question is in the pay of the Husky varsity, which would, it appears, go to any lengths to assure its victory.

Jefferson State O.C. Holds First Meet

by Mike Schuh

JSOC held their first "real" orienteering event on March 5 at Bear Creek Park in Medford. I went to Medford earlier in the week to make the map and help organize the event. The area is about the size of Woodland Park and is a flat city park alongside Bear Creek, a tributary of the Rouge River. It is mostly open fields, with some forest (also open) and some blackberry thickets along the creek. Just about perfect for an introductory event.

Dan and Terry Miller obtained an excellent base map from the city's engineering and parks departments (1:1200 with 2 foot contours, showing paths, vegetation boundaries, light poles, man holes, and most buildings—one wag asked if I merely colored it in). I was out mapping at 1:30pm on Wednesday (just 11 hours after arrival), and—after some self induced delays involving an incorrect file format—I had 50 color copies of the OCAD produced map and in my hand at 6:05pm Friday.

I gave an informal talk Thursday evening to a small but enthusiastic group, and had several meetings with key club members. Among them, Allyson Kelley called the various media to get some publicity. The event was listed in the Medford paper, and was on at least one television station.

Twelve people came and tried the two courses that I set out, including the meet staff of Allyson, Dan, and Terry. Of note was the appearance of Joe Hallet from Powers (in case your map of Oregon does not show Powers—a strong possibility—let me tell you that is west and then north from Grants Pass, some two hours away). The Medford locals were amazed and impressed that someone would actually drive for two hours—each way—just to run around a city park for a few minutes. Heh, soon they'll be doing the same, driving to Portland or the Bay Area for a one day orienteering event... Anyway, everyone was enthusiastic and plans were made for the next event, to be held in Bear Creek Park during Medford's Pear Blossom Days in early April.

After the meet, Dan, Terry, and I examined a nearby county park with an eye towards mapping it (I encouraged them to do so), and then drove to Ashland to do some quick field checking of Lithia Park. World famous, the park attracts some half million visitors every year. The park is immediately adjacent to Oregon Shakespeare Festival's Elizabethan Theater and makes for a pleasant stroll before or after a play. While the park might not make for the greatest in urban orienteering (it's long and skinny), I think it would be an excellent venue for a permanent course ("Trim" course), with the maps available from merchants in the plaza across the street from the park. We'll see what happens...

I enjoyed my all-too-brief visit to Medford, and would like to thank my hosts Dan and Terry for their hospitality, assistance, and enthusiasm. Thanks go to Allyson Kelley for her efforts, and to Brad Prior for obtaining a base map of Lithia and arranging a meeting room.

Orienteering Class

Mike Schuh will teach an introductory class on orienteering through the ASUW Experimental College. The class will be held at Discovery Park and will include outdoor exercises. For information regarding registration for the class, please call the Experimental College at 543-4375 (course number 4164, "Orienteering for Sport and Fun"). Mike may be reached at 783-3960. The class will begin Sunday, April 17th, and will continue Sundays through May 7th. There will be two sections of two hours each: one beginning at 10:00am, the other at 12:30pm. Tuition $20, less for seniors, students.
The same class will be offered through the Lynnwood Parks Department on Saturdays, beginning April 16th, from 10:00am until noon. At Lynndale Park; $22. Information: 771-4030

Class outline
I — introduction, map symbols, what to do when lost, map walk
II — map symbols review, line O' course
III — compass, pacing, compass course
IV — route choice, basic tactics, novice course, what next...

Orienteer in Spokane on Bloomsday Weekend

If you are going to Spokane for the Bloomsday Run May 1, you may want to take in an orienteering event at Manito Park on Saturday afternoon, April 30. Manito is a city park 1:5000 color map. The fee is $3 for members of any orienteering club. Registration will be from 1:00 to 3:00 p.m. To get to the park if you are eastbound on

I-90, take the Maple Street exit, go straight ahead at the bottom of the exit ramp and follow 4th Avenue (which runs parallel to I-90) east to Stevens, turn right (south) onto Stevens and get in the left lane for Grand Blvd. and follow Grand to the park.

Orienteer Involved In Rescue

by Mike Schuh

A truism in life is that we never really know what each day will bring—this serves to keep life interesting. On Saturday, February 26th, the day before the Washington State Intercollegiate Championships, I was expecting to meet Neil and Lael Smith at Lake Tradition to finish designing and setting the intermediate and advanced courses. What neither I nor about two dozen others did not expect was to be involved in a life saving rescue.

As Neil, Lael, and I were sorting through control markers at about 2:15 and preparing to leave the end of the road, a somewhat frantic teenage boy (Eric), ran by asking if anyone had a phone. A passerby did, and as we helped Eric place a cellular phone call to 911, we learned that his hiking companion (Kari, age 16) had fallen down a rock face and couldn't move. Two other hikers (Jim and Doyle), who were just preparing to set out on a day hike, offered their assistance. At my suggestion, Neil and Lael set out to place the markers on their courses. I called Neil later to tell him what happened.

(I should pause here in my narrative to share a bit of my background, as it is relevant to what follows. During my high school years, I took part in Explorer Scout Search and Rescue. This included my earliest introduction to off trail travel with map and compass (not quite orienteering, but vaguely similar), team leader training, a week at an Air Force survival school, other useful training, and many rewarding experiences. I also have a pilot's license, have taken several first aid courses, and have some rock climbing experience.)

Talking with Eric, Jim and I figured out that Kari was near a rock face 100+ meters east of the Talus Caves. Giving Jim and Doyle a copy of the Lake Tradition map, I sent them up the West Tiger Trail (it forms the eastern boundary of the map) with instructions that if they found her, one was to stay with her while the other came back down the same trail. This would save time for rescue teams later as they would not have to look for her. Shortly after they left (and less than 10 minutes from the phone call), an acting Battalion Chief (Kevin Taylor) from King County Fire District 10 arrived in a command van. He was followed in short order by a county aid van, another one from the City of Issaquah, and a county ladder truck. Why a ladder truck? Because it had a stokes litter, and neither of the aid cars did. I was impressed when the Issaquah medics pulled out of their van a pair of backpacks equipped with sleeping bags, food, water, oxygen bottle, and medic kits. It was after 2:30.

I rode to the trailhead shelter with the firefighters and Eric. At my suggestion, Eric led the two Issaquah medics up the Caves Trail. Now named Nook Trail, it is the most direct route to the Talus Caves and is the trail with which Eric was familiar. I led the five other firefighters up the West Tiger Trail, in part to meet Jim and Doyle and partly because, as an old road, it was the easiest route to carry the litter. Before we left I gave copies of the map to Eric and the Issaquah medics and left one with Chief Taylor (why not? I had a handful of them with me).

As my group approached the site, we were met by Jim and Doyle who confirmed the location and reported that a doctor hiking in the area was with Kari along with a few other hikers. We reached her at the same time that the medics did, and they got to work quickly and professionally. It was now after 3pm, or more then an hour since Kari had fallen. The medics' initial assessment was wrist injury, possible back injury, several lacerations, hypothermia from laying in the mud, and shock. Her vital signs were strong, and she was conscious the whole time. After securing her head in a neck brace, checking her over, and removing her wet clothes, she was placed inside a sleeping bag in the litter.

While the medics worked, I discussed with Kevin Jones, the firefighter in charge, what to do next. On the radio we could hear Chief Taylor first asking their dispatcher for an airlift and then giving us estimates of a helicopter's arrival time. With Kevin's concurrence, I asked Jim, Doyle, and another hiker to start a smoky fire, the idea being to give the helicopter crews an aid in finding us.

The chopper arrived at a little before 4:00. Earlier I had provided Chief Taylor with our position relative to Lake Tradition, the biggest landmark in the area ("1500 yards"—I could have said meters, it was an Army chopper after all—"on a bearing of 160"—all aircraft have magnetic compasses in them. I found out later that "1200 meters at 140" would have been more accurate). This and the fire must have helped, because after an initial pass along I-90, the chopper came straight to where we were. I had coached Kevin Jones on how to talk the chopper directly to us—"11 o'clock, 200 yards", meaning ahead of the pilot and to the left. Shortly, a flight medic specialist (Craig Davison) was lowered down to us. While the chopper hovered directly above, the firefighters and hikers working on Kari huddled over her to shield her from the 100 knot prop wash, which was cold and knocking branches loose (one large one just about hit the flight medic and me while we were figuring out where to lower the airlift's litter).

Under Craig's direction, we moved Kari down the hill to where she could be hoisted by the chopper, a site dictated by openings in the forest canopy. I assisted in setting up a rope arrangement that was readily understood by the other climbers in the group, primarily the doctor who handled the upper belay. During this time, the chopper set down to save fuel, doing so on the High

Page 5

Point Road, gumming up otherwise normal Saturday afternoon hiker traffic (the High Point trailhead is one of the most popular, not just in Washington State, but in the nation). After Kari had been transferred to the airlift's litter (but still on the county's backboard, which made the transfer easier), we called the chopper back and she was hoisted away, at about 4:30. It was quite a high for me, and I imagine for the others, to see her being pulled into the chopper door and to see the chopper head for Harborview Hospital.

I figured out later that Kari was in the clean, dry, warm emergency room in under 12 minutes, or before we had climbed back up to the trail where she originally lay, to say nothing of packing everything up and walking back down the hillside.

As we were leaving the site, members of Explorer Search and Rescue and Mountain Rescue arrived. Incredibly, they were not called until an hour after the initial call. Among them was Doug Caley, who has worked with Cascade OC in supporting some of our bigger meets with standby ESAR first aid crews. Coincidentally, one of them was the 1992 WIOL championships at Lake Tradition! They got to the scene just in time to watch Kari get hoisted up through the trees and spirited away. Had the airlift not been possible, they would have helped us carry her down the trail.

When I got back to the trailhead, I complimented my fellow rescuers on their efforts. It was 5:40, and I still had not set out any markers for the event the next day. But my thoughts were on how Kari was doing. An article in the March 12 Bellevue Journal American reported that Kari was hospitalized for four days, had metal rods implanted in her back for crushed vertebrae, wears a cast for a broken wrist, and—while recovering rapidly—presently has difficulty walking. It sounds like she will pull through very well.

Those of us involved at the scene reflected on how luck played a big part in her rescue. When she fell, she came to rest near a trail, which proved to be very convenient. It was a place that Eric could describe well enough to Jim and me that we had confidence in her location, which helped plan later efforts. The weather was dry, though cool, and there were several hours of daylight remaining. The trailhead was within cellular phone range (there is a pay phone at the restrooms, but few hikers know it is there), and a cellular phone was available. Many hikers in the Seattle area have first aid training and travel prepared for emergencies (true elsewhere, I'm sure) and a doctor was nearby. The various agencies involved—King County fire, Issaquah fire, Army MAST, Department of Natural Resources, ESAR, and MRC—have the ability to share radio communications, which was most important when the chopper was trying to find us among the trees. How many outdoor accidents are not so lucky? Wet as she was from landing in a creek, it is questionable if Kari would have survived the night.

After things quieted down, I decided that I needed to place at least a few markers. With the gate open, I made arrangements with Chief Taylor regarding locking it, and drove to the west end of the plateau. There, in what little light was available from the city lights, I put out two markers, one on a spur that Debbie Newell, who vetted the course the next morning, noticed was misplaced (she moved it—thanks!). As I drove to place one more, I thought about what I was doing and what I had just experienced earlier in the day. I decided that walking alone in the dark without a flashlight while exhausted and with no one knowing where I was going was not particularly smart. I drove home, belatedly finished the course design on paper, and slept soundly. Up early, I hung the last four of my markers, having decided to "borrow" some controls from the other courses. It seemed to work out OK, but it would have been a better course (perhaps) if I had been able to walk around Saturday afternoon...

There are several lessons from this experience. The first, I think, is to be prepared for and contemplate the unexpected. How many of us, as we go out to set courses or to field check, seriously consider the possibility and consequences of injuring ourselves? Knowing where we are on the map can give us a false sense of security, which can lead to stupid actions. I can remember the first time I set courses at Drunken Charlie Lake (on a black and white map in 1984)—no one really knew I was out there, much less exactly where I was. If I had fallen...

On the positive side, there are a lot of really wonderful hikers out there who are willing—and able—to contribute valuable assistance in an emergency such as this. Orienteers of all ages, but especially high school boys and girls, can contribute their navigational abilities. One way for students to do so is to join ESAR (adults are also welcome). For more information on ESAR, call 296-3833. I gained a lot from my involvement with ESAR, and I would encourage others to consider joining and going through the training. Hopefully, it will rarely be needed.

Membership Report

by Aaron Charlop

Hello all. This is my first report since taking over the job of Membership Coordinator from Harvey Friedman. I wish to start this report with a note of thanks to Harvey for all his help in getting me situated in this position. I would have been lost with his help.

We have taken the change of coordinators as an opportunity to institute a few changes in the way that the membership database is kept. Most of the changes are invisible to you the member, but it is hoped that they will make the management of the database more efficient. The major change has been that the database has been changed from being in dBase on a CP/M machine to being a Filemaker Pro file on a Mac. We have also added some new fields to the database. For instance, the database has a field for the electronic mail address of members who have access to computer mail networks. Below you will find a list of E-mail addresses

Page 6

for several members. If you would like your electronic address included in the database, send an e-mail message to me at the address listed below.

Many of you will notice another change that has been instituted with this issue of the newsletter. For those members whose membership has expired there is a brightly colored insert in your newsletter informing you that this will be your last newsletter until your membership is renewed. The insert conatins a membership application that has the current database information already entered into it. All that is needed is for you to make any necessary corrections and mail the insert to the club with a check to renew your membership. Also, all labels on the newsletter indicate the date when your membership in COC expires.

Now to thank all the renewing members of COC, Don Atkinson and Family, Mark Bingham and Family, the Croteau Family, Ardis Dull, Larry Farrar and Family, Rick and Linda Hood, Ken Knapp and Family, Michael Mahoney, Boy Scout Troop 398, Robert and Sandy Miller, Carl and Linda Moore, John Nitavdy, Knut and Ralph Olson, Walbridge J. We would also like to welcome several new members to COC; Edwin Armistead and Family, Ann Brooks, Ron Brown, Charles and Lynn Butler, Jim and Rhonda Gorgan, David Korthals, and Terry Loving.

WSOA honors top WIOL runners

The Washington State Orienteering Association has granted to the top boys and girls in this year's Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League memberships in local clubs and in the United States Orienteering Federation. Selection was based on their placing in the championships and their season standings, as well as their perceived level of interest and promise in orienteering. Besides getting Bearing 315 and Orienteering North America, their memberships will give them discounts at local orienteering events and at national Class A meets. It is hoped that this will encourage them to continue their involvement with the sport, and help them improve their skills. These individuals (along with George Alina and Crystal Rollings) will also be recommended to the USOF national team for inclusion on the national development squad. Congratulations to:

Sammamish News

Dave Lilly

SAMM's “in the works department” includes looking into some updating of maps, possibly with a grouped press run this summer. Pioneer Park and Luther Burbank Park on Mercer Island, and an expanded Robinswood Park in south-east Bellevue, are the projects we’re considering. We have yet to delve into "O-CAD" in a big way, but are researching purchasing a copy for club use. I feel the need to editorialize about meets. I’d really like to see more people getting out there and helping put them on. It can't be said enough. We have a local core group that does 90% of the work involved in putting on meets. It would really help if every club member could volunteer to do something at least once a year. We can't hope to hold A-meets, or other large functions, if we can only rely on a few people who have always done everything.
* For the rest of the seasoned orienteers, I find we can be an ornery lot. Lets keep are criticisms constructive and not destructive. Course setters need feedback, but lets make this a useful interface of information. We beg people to take on tasks, and then use it as a chance to whine about a bad performance. Let’s cut these tenderfoot course setters a break.

Field Notes

Rick Hood

Rick & Eileen Breseman are building a new home in Malbey on a 5 acre parcel. Rick has been doing much of the logging and is offering anyone, "cut, unseasoned, ready-to-go" firewood. Give Rick a call for driving instructions. (Is there a micro-O map and event in the works, a la the Fritz course of last summer; stay tuned.) Also with Ryan & Kelsey Breseman and Ben and Peter Sprugel training hard can a national string-O champion from Washington State be that far off? Cascade's Sept. 10/11 Cle Elum meet has been upgraded. On Saturday, there will be the first ever official US Short Course Championships which will consist of two sub-events, one in the late morning and the other in the afternoon. Each sub-event course will be about 1/3 to 1/2 as long as normal course. Your times for both sub-events are com-

Page 7

bined to determine your finish standards. We are please to announce that participants will earn A-meet USOF ranking points, not just credit days. On Sunday, Cascade will offer a traditional A meet including a brown (or short distance advanced) course and quite possibly a Elite course too (a super Blue course, if you will). Course designer Peter Golde, course vetter Rick Breseman, and course consultant Mike Fritz are excited; we hope you are as well.
The third annual Drunken Charlie Lake Rogaine, slatted for May 14 will be run much like last year's successful team event. The 6 hour course will open at 11am and run through 5pm with the 3 hour event having starts options on the ½ hour from 11:30 through 1:30. To add some freshness to the event a new event design team of Paul Magus and Bill Cusworth will work with some of the old guys (myself as MD and Aaron as chief grill chef.) So start lining up your partners now for the DCL classic. The event fee of $10 will include post-race food. We will try to hold our trail and natural bridge building project in June, close to national trail day. We will announce the date in the next issue and we hope you will join us and help demonstrate that orienteers do more than just run in the woods.
Sammamish's Bob Reddick, was featured in the current International Rogaine Federation Newsletter. Bob and Pat Reddick also hosted Peter Taylor, the IRF president, last month for a few days, showing him the sights and some "potential super Rogaine terrain" down by Mt. Saint Helen. Speaking a famous and Sammamish folks, John Rogers and his '93 Mountain Goat Event received a very nice write-up in the current Orienteering North America. A very well deserved congratulation John!
The Alpine Trails bulletin board served the orienteering community well during the previous year, providing news about local and national orienteering events. Unfortunately, it has now shut down for at least the next six months. The US Champs in Alaska is just around the corner (time-wise and travel-wise, especially if you check with our "Air Travel Discount Chairman, Bruce McAlister). It should be a super 3 days of A meets with a Rogaine on the following Saturday. So, don't miss this one if you can and help us support Dan Ellsworth and our Alaskan Pacific Northwest Regional family.
OK, you don't want to fly and are looking for a fantastic event, drive up to the Kamloops area for the 1st ever North American Rogaine Championships. Like last year they will have a full 24 hour course and a split 12 hour option. (Both options start at noon on Saturday with the later stopping at 8pm and restarting at 8am on Sunday.) OK, if you haven't been to Kamloops for a Sage Rogaine before, you are really in for a treat!!! See what I mean; drive or carpool up with us for the Sage club McQueen Lake event this April 1-4, just around the corner. If you haven't been to Kamloops, some would say you just haven't orienteered on "friendly and exciting terrain". Vivien Fritz said don't be surprised to see some new faces at the upcoming Cascade Farrel McWhirter Park meet March 27th. The event is being co-sponsored the Redmond Parks and Recreation Department; announcement, the P&R booklet, went out to everyone in the greater Redmond/Woodinville area. (If you would like to help insure that newcomer have a great first experience, I suspect Vivien would love your help; call her at 481-5877.)
And a "best-of-luck, but doubt-that-you'll-need-it" to the Husky Orienteering Club who are going back east in early April to defend their Intercollegiate Championships. Much success everyone!!! Lastly, hats off to Mike Schuh and Ardis Dull and the rest of the 1993-94 WIOL staff; it was indeed a successful season. Don't take my word, just ask those than ran. Here's looking forward to another super next season.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?
Cascade Orienteering Club, Sammamish Orienteering Club, Ellensburg Orienteering Club, Sacajawea Orienteers, and Husky O.C. are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, and Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?
The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Winter O' Series

During the WIOL season, Cascade held a competition for the general public. On the advanced course at each meet, individuals received points based on their finish order. At the end of the season, each person's four best scores were added together to determine final placings. In the men's competition, Rune Hoel (Husky O' Club), a UW student from Norway, edged out defending champion Eric Bone (Husky O' Club), who fell to third while Mark Howlett (Sammamish O' Club) retained his second place position. Eric was the course setter at the final meet, so had no opportunity to improve his position. Paul Magnus (Cascade O' Club) finished just 2 points behind Eric. In the women's division, Debbie Newell (Cascade O' Club) successfully defended her title, finishing in 8th place overall. Marti Anderson (COC) vaulted to second place by virtue of her winning run at the Kelso meet and Sandy Kish Miller (COC) was close behind in third place.
Events used were: 1. Fort Stielacom, 2. Bog Slog (St. Edwards), 3. NAD Park, 4. Marymoor Park, 5. Priest Point, 6. Bridle Trails, 7. Kelso, 8. Seward Park.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

Inspired by the success of Eric Bone and the UW team at last year’s US Intercollegiate Championships, I suggested that Cascade OC host a state intercollegiate event this year. This was accepted by the COC board, especially when I said I would be the meet director. The date was set to follow the WIOL championships and to be a month or so before the national championships. Lake Tradition was chosen as being most suitable, with challenging terrain and a large area.

Intercollegiate scoring was derived from the national rules, with several simplifications: the Washington State event would be just one day instead of two, both men and women would be on the same course, team scores would be the total time of the fastest three finishers from each school, and anyone attending a Washington State post-secondary school full time would be eligible.

There were 14 participants from six schools. To no one’s surprise, the UW team, comprised of half of last year’s national champion team, took the first three places and the team title. As no other school had three finishers, second place went to Western Washington University, the only school with two finishers. Third place went to Pacific Lutheran University.

Among the individuals, Rune Hoel edged out Eric Bone for the men. For the women, Lisa Nordlund and high school class mate Ellen Boomer (now attending different colleges) were the only entrants with Lisa, a top WIOL participant a few years back, getting to the finish faster.

Of the 14 participants, four (Eric, Tyler, Lisa, and Ellen) are WIOL “alumni”. Dan Waugh, ever the promoter of orienteering (and founder of WIOL) brought out four members of the UW’s Russia and Eastern Europe program. And, to round out the international flavor of the proceedings, there were three Norwegian students as well—Rune, Kjell, and Harald.

All in all, the meet went quite well. The team and women's competition might not have been keen, but this is the first year for this event, and I hope that it grow in the future. My thanks to all who took part and to the meet staff: Dave Petty, registration; Marti Anderson and Terry Loving, timing; Jackie Ritmire, course 1 design and setting; Neil and Lael Smith, courses 2 and 3 design and setting; Bruce McAlister for providing refreshments; Debbie Newell, awards and vetting; Fred Schmitt, vetting; and Eric Bone, Bill Cusworth, Rune Hoel, Lisa Nordlund, Joseph Ho, Seth Brothers, and Jackie Ritmire for picking up markers. Thank you!

The Twelfth Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League Championships were held at Seattle’s Seward Park on February 12th. Competition was keen on the A course, and on the B course as well. Bremerton High School took the team honors, partly due to their large numbers (a bus load...) but also to Terry Bryant’s coaching.

Last year’s champions, David Thatcher and Rachell Tharp, were both seniors, leaving this year's title open. This year’s boy’s championships did seem to echo last year’s, however: Nicholas Bone beat Fred Schmitt, both ahead of Chris Schreiner and David Stainsby (last year, David placed ahead of Chris). That was on the A course. On the reinstated B course, rookie Chris Hooyman blew past everyone and won with a margin of nearly seven minutes. Second place Randy Coe improved mightily from last year and finished more than a minute ahead of teammate George Alina.
For the girls, Maia Wright was the only one to attempt the A course, earning her the title of champion

(she placed in the middle of an otherwise all male field). On the B course, Joan Maza was the first girl, placing fifth overall. Christine Matter and Kari Jones took second and third, with Kari having just 30 seconds over teammate Heather Daniels.

For the middle school students, Derek Schmitt repeated as champion. Second was Lael Smith, and Nathan Schmitt was third. Last year, Lael ran in a public category on the same course as the Nathan, but not quite as quickly. Sadly, this year Nathan copied the wrong set of control codes onto his control card, and lost a lot of time on the course. On the bright side, both Nathan and Lael are seventh graders, so they can look forward to five more WIOL championships and, presumably, a few rematches.
No girls took part in the middle school competition.

The only time Angela and Nicholas Tallent took part in WIOL events this season was at the championship where, uncontested, they became the grade school champions. Last year they finished in a tie; this year Angela (a former national age group champion) was faster than her little brother. I suspect that she won't be able to do that too much longer...

There were a few problems at the championships. The worst was Chris Hooyman getting a B course map instead of one for the A course. I allowed him to run the A course later (which he completed in about 41 minutes, two minutes better than Nicholas Bone), but upon reconsideration decided that this wouldn't be fair to the others who competed on the A course. A true sportsman, Chris accepted first place on the B course—and he'll be back next year as a senior.

There was some confusion as to which course was for which participants; next year we will try to be more clear about this (Nathan Schmitt's problems were a result of this). Another problem came from the design of the WIOL B course. The best route to control 1 passed near control 5, which proved to be too great a temptation for a couple of participants. At control 3 on the B course (which was control 5 on the A course) control cards were checked and two competitors had visited controls out order, which is against the rules. A slight change in the design of the course might have prevented this problem.

In general, the event was a good one, and I would like to thank the many individuals who helped make it so: assistant meet director Paul Magnus; mappers Bill Cusworth and Eric Bone (Eric, 1991 and 1992 WIOL champion, also designed and set the courses); starters Marti Anderson, Walt Estep, and Toko Magnus; finish crew David Keyes, Toko Magnus, Mark Howlett, Rick Hood; registration workers Aaron Charlop, Harvey Friedman, and Jim Ley; awards by Debbie Newell; WIOL registration Ardis Dull and Mark Anderson; refreshments by Bruce McAlister; and general assistance by Rick Hood, Tric Charlop, and Margie Bone. I hope I mentioned everyone...
Thank you everyone, and we'll see you in November for next year’s league!

"No Dan O'Briens here!" shouted Paul McClung as he vaulted over a moss-encrusted deadfall and disappeared into a swamp, while leading Team REECAS to stunning near upset of the national champion UW Husky Orienteering team in the Washington Intercollegiate Championship. The REECAS team came out of nowhere in the narrowest finish since the inception of this classic event, with McClung followed by closely by Ben Hanson, Dobek Pater and Rob Smurr.

The medal winning performance was especially satisfying, in the view of the fact that the main opposition, the Husky team, not only fielded the defending national intercollegiate champion but had added a Norwegian ringer, who eked out a win on the 5-kilometer course on the Lake Tradition plateau above the once sleepy town of Issaquah.

When interviewed by CNN after the competition, the team coach, Dan Waugh, who also ran, lauded his runners. "They're compete on a level of those with years more experience," he said, "and in rough terrain." Elaborating, with reference to his patch and torn orienteering suit, gaping scratches in his thighs and muddy ankles, Waugh went on, "Orienteering is like a metaphor for being a program chair at a university—a struggle with limited resources, thorny problems, and a lot of s--t. If it weren't for the inspiration of this outstanding group of graduate students...."

Team REECAS has truly an uphill fight in its quest for the championship. Recalcitrant meet officials tried to award its medal to another school whose "team" consisted of one individual. Worse, one of the team’s outstanding performers, Lorca Fitschen, was sidelined by an end-of-quarter term-paper assigned by an East European Historian notorious for his demands on students. When reached at her home, Lorca commented, "It was like a well-aimed blow to the knee with an aluminum truncheon. I kept asking, why me, why me? Why now?" Rumor has it that the professor in question is in the pay of the Husky varsity, which would, it appears, go to any lengths to assure its victory.
In a related development, the Jamaican 4-man bob-

Jefferson State O.C. Holds First Meet

JSOC held their first "real" orienteering event on March 5 at Bear Creek Park in Medford. I went to Medford earlier in the week to make the map and help organize the event. The area is about the size of Woodland Park and is a flat city park alongside Bear Creek, a tributary of the Rouge River. It is mostly open fields, with some forest (also open) and some blackberry thickets along the creek. Just about perfect for an introductory event.

Dan and Terry Miller obtained an excellent base map from the city's engineering and parks departments (1:1200 with 2 foot contours, showing paths, vegetation boundaries, light poles, man holes, and most buildings—one wag asked if I merely colored it in). I was out mapping at 1:30pm on Wednesday (just 11 hours after arrival), and—after some self induced delays involving an incorrect file format—I had 50 color copies of the OCAD produced map and in my hand at 6:05pm Friday.

I gave an informal talk Thursday evening to a small but enthusiastic group, and had several meetings with key club members. Among them, Allyson Kelley called the various media to get some publicity. The event was listed in the Medford paper, and was on at least one television station.

Twelve people came and tried the two courses that I set out, including the meet staff of Allyson, Dan, and Terry. Of note was the appearance of Joe Hallet from Powers (in case your map of Oregon does not show Powers—a strong possibility—let me tell you that is west and then north from Grants Pass, some two hours away). The Medford locals were amazed and impressed that someone would actually drive for two hours—each way—just to run around a city park for a few minutes. Heh, soon they'll be doing the same, driving to Portland or the Bay Area for a one day orienteering event... Anyway, everyone was enthusiastic and plans were made for the next event, to be held in Bear Creek Park during Medford's Pear Blossom Days in early April.

After the meet, Dan, Terry, and I examined a nearby county park with an eye towards mapping it (I encouraged them to do so), and then drove to Ashland to do some quick field checking of Lithia Park. World famous, the park attracts some half million visitors every year. The park is immediately adjacent to Oregon Shakespeare Festival's Elizabethan Theater and makes for a pleasant stroll before or after a play. While the park might not make for the greatest in urban orienteering (it's long and skinny), I think it would be an excellent venue for a permanent course ("Trim" course), with the maps available from merchants in the plaza across the street from the park. We'll see what happens...

I enjoyed my all-too-brief visit to Medford, and would like to thank my hosts Dan and Terry for their hospitality, assistance, and enthusiasm. Thanks go to Allyson Kelley for her efforts, and to Brad Prior for obtaining a base map of Lithia and arranging a meeting room.

Orienteer in Spokane on Bloomsday Weekend

If you are going to Spokane for the Bloomsday Run May 1, you may want to take in an orienteering event at Manito Park on Saturday afternoon, April 30. Manito is a city park 1:5000 color map. The fee is $3 for members of any orienteering club. Registration will be from 1:00 to 3:00 p.m. To get to the park if you are eastbound on

A truism in life is that we never really know what each day will bring—this serves to keep life interesting. On Saturday, February 26th, the day before the Washington State Intercollegiate Championships, I was expecting to meet Neil and Lael Smith at Lake Tradition to finish designing and setting the intermediate and advanced courses. What neither I nor about two dozen others did not expect was to be involved in a life saving rescue.

As Neil, Lael, and I were sorting through control markers at about 2:15 and preparing to leave the end of the road, a somewhat frantic teenage boy (Eric), ran by asking if anyone had a phone. A passerby did, and as we helped Eric place a cellular phone call to 911, we learned that his hiking companion (Kari, age 16) had fallen down a rock face and couldn't move. Two other hikers (Jim and Doyle), who were just preparing to set out on a day hike, offered their assistance. At my suggestion, Neil and Lael set out to place the markers on their courses. I called Neil later to tell him what happened.

(I should pause here in my narrative to share a bit of my background, as it is relevant to what follows. During my high school years, I took part in Explorer Scout Search and Rescue. This included my earliest introduction to off trail travel with map and compass (not quite orienteering, but vaguely similar), team leader training, a week at an Air Force survival school, other useful training, and many rewarding experiences. I also have a pilot's license, have taken several first aid courses, and have some rock climbing experience.)

Talking with Eric, Jim and I figured out that Kari was near a rock face 100+ meters east of the Talus Caves. Giving Jim and Doyle a copy of the Lake Tradition map, I sent them up the West Tiger Trail (it forms the eastern boundary of the map) with instructions that if they found her, one was to stay with her while the other came back down the same trail. This would save time for rescue teams later as they would not have to look for her. Shortly after they left (and less than 10 minutes from the phone call), an acting Battalion Chief (Kevin Taylor) from King County Fire District 10 arrived in a command van. He was followed in short order by a county aid van, another one from the City of Issaquah, and a county ladder truck. Why a ladder truck? Because it had a stokes litter, and neither of the aid cars did. I was impressed when the Issaquah medics pulled out of their van a pair of backpacks equipped with sleeping bags, food, water, oxygen bottle, and medic kits. It was after 2:30.

I rode to the trailhead shelter with the firefighters and Eric. At my suggestion, Eric led the two Issaquah medics up the Caves Trail. Now named Nook Trail, it is the most direct route to the Talus Caves and is the trail with which Eric was familiar. I led the five other firefighters up the West Tiger Trail, in part to meet Jim and Doyle and partly because, as an old road, it was the easiest route to carry the litter. Before we left I gave copies of the map to Eric and the Issaquah medics and left one with Chief Taylor (why not? I had a handful of them with me).

As my group approached the site, we were met by Jim and Doyle who confirmed the location and reported that a doctor hiking in the area was with Kari along with a few other hikers. We reached her at the same time that the medics did, and they got to work quickly and professionally. It was now after 3pm, or more then an hour since Kari had fallen. The medics' initial assessment was wrist injury, possible back injury, several lacerations, hypothermia from laying in the mud, and shock. Her vital signs were strong, and she was conscious the whole time. After securing her head in a neck brace, checking her over, and removing her wet clothes, she was placed inside a sleeping bag in the litter.

While the medics worked, I discussed with Kevin Jones, the firefighter in charge, what to do next. On the radio we could hear Chief Taylor first asking their dispatcher for an airlift and then giving us estimates of a helicopter's arrival time. With Kevin's concurrence, I asked Jim, Doyle, and another hiker to start a smoky fire, the idea being to give the helicopter crews an aid in finding us.

The chopper arrived at a little before 4:00. Earlier I had provided Chief Taylor with our position relative to Lake Tradition, the biggest landmark in the area ("1500 yards"—I could have said meters, it was an Army chopper after all—"on a bearing of 160"—all aircraft have magnetic compasses in them. I found out later that "1200 meters at 140" would have been more accurate). This and the fire must have helped, because after an initial pass along I-90, the chopper came straight to where we were. I had coached Kevin Jones on how to talk the chopper directly to us—"11 o'clock, 200 yards", meaning ahead of the pilot and to the left. Shortly, a flight medic specialist (Craig Davison) was lowered down to us. While the chopper hovered directly above, the firefighters and hikers working on Kari huddled over her to shield her from the 100 knot prop wash, which was cold and knocking branches loose (one large one just about hit the flight medic and me while we were figuring out where to lower the airlift's litter).

Under Craig's direction, we moved Kari down the hill to where she could be hoisted by the chopper, a site dictated by openings in the forest canopy. I assisted in setting up a rope arrangement that was readily understood by the other climbers in the group, primarily the doctor who handled the upper belay. During this time, the chopper set down to save fuel, doing so on the High

Point Road, gumming up otherwise normal Saturday afternoon hiker traffic (the High Point trailhead is one of the most popular, not just in Washington State, but in the nation). After Kari had been transferred to the airlift's litter (but still on the county's backboard, which made the transfer easier), we called the chopper back and she was hoisted away, at about 4:30. It was quite a high for me, and I imagine for the others, to see her being pulled into the chopper door and to see the chopper head for Harborview Hospital.

I figured out later that Kari was in the clean, dry, warm emergency room in under 12 minutes, or before we had climbed back up to the trail where she originally lay, to say nothing of packing everything up and walking back down the hillside.

As we were leaving the site, members of Explorer Search and Rescue and Mountain Rescue arrived. Incredibly, they were not called until an hour after the initial call. Among them was Doug Caley, who has worked with Cascade OC in supporting some of our bigger meets with standby ESAR first aid crews. Coincidentally, one of them was the 1992 WIOL championships at Lake Tradition! They got to the scene just in time to watch Kari get hoisted up through the trees and spirited away. Had the airlift not been possible, they would have helped us carry her down the trail.

When I got back to the trailhead, I complimented my fellow rescuers on their efforts. It was 5:40, and I still had not set out any markers for the event the next day. But my thoughts were on how Kari was doing. An article in the March 12 Bellevue Journal American reported that Kari was hospitalized for four days, had metal rods implanted in her back for crushed vertebrae, wears a cast for a broken wrist, and—while recovering rapidly—presently has difficulty walking. It sounds like she will pull through very well.

Those of us involved at the scene reflected on how luck played a big part in her rescue. When she fell, she came to rest near a trail, which proved to be very convenient. It was a place that Eric could describe well enough to Jim and me that we had confidence in her location, which helped plan later efforts. The weather was dry, though cool, and there were several hours of daylight remaining. The trailhead was within cellular phone range (there is a pay phone at the restrooms, but few hikers know it is there), and a cellular phone was available. Many hikers in the Seattle area have first aid training and travel prepared for emergencies (true elsewhere, I'm sure) and a doctor was nearby. The various agencies involved—King County fire, Issaquah fire, Army MAST, Department of Natural Resources, ESAR, and MRC—have the ability to share radio communications, which was most important when the chopper was trying to find us among the trees. How many outdoor accidents are not so lucky? Wet as she was from landing in a creek, it is questionable if Kari would have survived the night.

After things quieted down, I decided that I needed to place at least a few markers. With the gate open, I made arrangements with Chief Taylor regarding locking it, and drove to the west end of the plateau. There, in what little light was available from the city lights, I put out two markers, one on a spur that Debbie Newell, who vetted the course the next morning, noticed was misplaced (she moved it—thanks!). As I drove to place one more, I thought about what I was doing and what I had just experienced earlier in the day. I decided that walking alone in the dark without a flashlight while exhausted and with no one knowing where I was going was not particularly smart. I drove home, belatedly finished the course design on paper, and slept soundly. Up early, I hung the last four of my markers, having decided to "borrow" some controls from the other courses. It seemed to work out OK, but it would have been a better course (perhaps) if I had been able to walk around Saturday afternoon...

There are several lessons from this experience. The first, I think, is to be prepared for and contemplate the unexpected. How many of us, as we go out to set courses or to field check, seriously consider the possibility and consequences of injuring ourselves? Knowing where we are on the map can give us a false sense of security, which can lead to stupid actions. I can remember the first time I set courses at Drunken Charlie Lake (on a black and white map in 1984)—no one really knew I was out there, much less exactly where I was. If I had fallen...

On the positive side, there are a lot of really wonderful hikers out there who are willing—and able—to contribute valuable assistance in an emergency such as this. Orienteers of all ages, but especially high school boys and girls, can contribute their navigational abilities. One way for students to do so is to join ESAR (adults are also welcome). For more information on ESAR, call 296-3833. I gained a lot from my involvement with ESAR, and I would encourage others to consider joining and going through the training. Hopefully, it will rarely be needed.

Hello all. This is my first report since taking over the job of Membership Coordinator from Harvey Friedman. I wish to start this report with a note of thanks to Harvey for all his help in getting me situated in this position. I would have been lost with his help.

We have taken the change of coordinators as an opportunity to institute a few changes in the way that the membership database is kept. Most of the changes are invisible to you the member, but it is hoped that they will make the management of the database more efficient. The major change has been that the database has been changed from being in dBase on a CP/M machine to being a Filemaker Pro file on a Mac. We have also added some new fields to the database. For instance, the database has a field for the electronic mail address of members who have access to computer mail networks. Below you will find a list of E-mail addresses
for several members. If you would like your electronic address included in the database, send an e-mail message to me at the address listed below.

Many of you will notice another change that has been instituted with this issue of the newsletter. For those members whose membership has expired there is a brightly colored insert in your newsletter informing you that this will be your last newsletter until your membership is renewed. The insert conatins a membership application that has the current database information already entered into it. All that is needed is for you to make any necessary corrections and mail the insert to the club with a check to renew your membership. Also, all labels on the newsletter indicate the date when your membership in COC expires.

Now to thank all the renewing members of COC, Don Atkinson and Family, Mark Bingham and Family, the Croteau Family, Ardis Dull, Larry Farrar and Family, Rick and Linda Hood, Ken Knapp and Family, Michael Mahoney, Boy Scout Troop 398, Robert and Sandy Miller, Carl and Linda Moore, John Nitavdy, Knut and Ralph Olson, Walbridge J. Powell, Michael L. Schuh, Roger Sharp, Neil Smith and Family, and Ross Stevens. We would also like to welcome several new members to COC; Edwin Armistead and Family, Ann Brooks, Ron Brown, Charles and Lynn Butler, Jim and Rhonda Gorgan, David Korthals, and Terry Loving.

The Washington State Orienteering Association has granted to the top boys and girls in this year's Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League memberships in local clubs and in the United States Orienteering Federation. Selection was based on their placing in the championships and their season standings, as well as their perceived level of interest and promise in orienteering. Besides getting Bearing 315 and Orienteering North America, their memberships will give them discounts at local orienteering events and at national Class A meets. It is hoped that this will encourage them to continue their involvement with the sport, and help them improve their skills. These individuals (along with George Alina and Crystal Rollings) will also be recommended to the USOF national team for inclusion on the national development squad. SAMM's “in the works department” includes looking into some updating of maps, possibly with a grouped press run this summer. Pioneer Park and Luther Burbank Park on Mercer Island, and an expanded Robinswood Park in south-east Bellevue, are the projects we’re considering. We have yet to delve into "O-CAD" in a big way, but are researching purchasing a copy for club use. I feel the need to editorialize about meets. I’d really like to see more people getting out there and helping put them on. It can't be said enough. We have a local core group that does 90% of the work involved in putting on meets. It would really help if every club member could volunteer to do something at least once a year. We can't hope to hold A-meets, or other large functions, if we can only rely on a few people who have always done everything. For the rest of the seasoned orienteers, I find we can be an ornery lot. Lets keep are criticisms constructive and not destructive. Course setters need feedback, but lets make this a useful interface of information. We beg people to take on tasks, and then use it as a chance to whine about a bad performance. Let’s cut these tenderfoot course setters a break.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs? Cascade Orienteering Club, Sammamish Orienteering Club, Ellensburg Orienteering Club, Sacajawea Orienteers, and Husky O.C. are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, and Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need? The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Course four was a slightly unusual event: line orienteering. Competitors follow a line drawn on the map and punch any controls they encounter along the line. No control codes are given, only the control number. The goal is to follow the line as accurately as possible. You must find all the controls to finish the course.

Note: Since nobody found Control #3, control placement was suspected. However, further investigation showed that the placement was correct and that all participants made the same navigational error on that control.

The winter classic: standing between you and the finish are 40 controls, 3 hours, and about 1000 rootstocks. Light rain didn't deter nearly a hundred people from attending this time-honored Score-O/Rogaine format event. Carl Moore's course didn't disappoint them either, with all the features we come to know and love: the control that's off the map, the rootstock in the middle of nowhere, and the thick, wet undergrowth that makes this event live up to its name. 2800 points were distributed among the 40 markers.

Season standings were calculated for the WIOL A and B courses using the ratio between each participant's time and the average of the first three finishers. Mispunches and not finishing earned zero points. For each participant, the best four scores were used to calculate the final standings. It should be noted in passing that North Kitsap's Fred Schmitt won each and every regular season meet that he ran. Events were, in order: 1. Fort Stielacom, 2. Bog Slog (St. Edwards), 3. NAD Park, 4. Marymoor Park, 5. Priest Point, 6. Bridle Trails, 7. Kelso.

During the WIOL season, Cascade held a competition for the general public. On the advanced course at each meet, individuals received points based on their finish order. At the end of the season, each person's four best scores were added together to determine final placings. In the men's competition, Rune Hoel (Husky O' Club), a UW student from Norway, edged out defending champion Eric Bone (Husky O' Club), who fell to third while Mark Howlett (Sammamish O' Club) retained his second place position. Eric was the course setter at the final meet, so had no opportunity to improve his position. Paul Magnus (Cascade O' Club) finished just 2 points behind Eric. In the women's division, Debbie Newell (Cascade O' Club) successfully defended her title, finishing in 8th place overall. Marti Anderson (COC) vaulted to second place by virtue of her winning run at the Kelso meet and Sandy Kish Miller (COC) was close behind in third place. Events used were: 1. Fort Stielacom, 2. Bog Slog (St. Edwards), 3. NAD Park, 4. Marymoor Park, 5. Priest Point, 6. Bridle Trails, 7. Kelso, 8. Seward Park.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
